behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels
behaviors.justification: This tests the shape and contents of the request payload we build. A mock of the API client, with an assertion on call arguments, directly verifies that all taxonomy labels are included before the request is sent. No I/O or external infrastructure is needed—we're testing our request-building code in isolation. The real failure (request missing labels) is caught by checking what arguments the code passes to the mocked API method.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API client. Call the classification function with a document and the configured taxonomy. Assert that the mock's call arguments include all expected taxonomy labels in the payload. Verify the presence of each label in the serialized request.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns "unknown" instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller
behaviors.justification: This tests the module's edge-case handling of invalid API responses. A mock configured to return an out-of-taxonomy label directly tests the validation logic. The real failure (forwarding an invalid label) is caught by asserting the module returns 'unknown'. No external API calls or I/O needed—this is pure validation logic in the module.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API to return a label value not in the configured taxonomy (e.g., 'garbage' when taxonomy is ['legal', 'technical', 'financial']). Call the classification function and assert it returns 'unknown', not the unexpected label from the API.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls
behaviors.justification: A pure mock would always return the same value, hiding whether the real LLM produces non-deterministic outputs. To verify the invariant that repeated calls yield identical results, we need to call the actual (non-deterministic) LLM twice. A Unit-level mock with side_effect could test that caching is implemented, but cannot test the invariant in the real system where the LLM may return different labels on separate calls. The real failure is exposed when the same document is sent to a real LLM twice and different classifications are returned—only an Agentic-level test catches this.
behaviors.test_description: Call the classification function twice with the same document, using the real external LLM API (not a mock). Assert that both calls return the identical label. This reveals whether caching is implemented and whether the real LLM's non-determinism is either controlled (e.g., temperature=0) or mitigated by caching.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget
behaviors.justification: This tests the module's token-budget enforcement logic. A mock configured to return various token_count values directly tests the code path for budget violations (e.g., error handling, truncation, retry). The real failure (code ignores or incorrectly handles token counts exceeding budget) is caught at the Unit level by asserting the module's response to over-budget scenarios. No real API call or subprocess needed—we are testing the validation and error-handling logic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API to return token_count values both under and over the configured budget (e.g., budget=1000, mock returns 500, then 1500). Call the classification function and assert that when tokens exceed budget, the module either raises an appropriate error, truncates, retries, or otherwise handles the violation according to spec.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high (429), the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller
behaviors.justification: This tests the module's retry and backoff logic. A mock configured to raise a rate-limit error (HTTP 429 or equivalent) on the first call and succeed on the second directly verifies the retry behavior. The real failure (code returns an error instead of retrying) is caught by asserting eventual success. We can mock time (e.g., using freezegun) to avoid slow tests. No actual HTTP server or rate limiting needed—we're testing the retry logic in isolation.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API client to raise a rate-limit exception on the first call, then return a valid result on the second call. Call the classification function and assert it eventually succeeds and returns the correct label. Verify using call_count or call tracking that the mock was called at least twice, confirming the retry occurred.
